text: I have invented this loop passer to perform various assisted two-port appendectomy.
The surgeon demonstrates a loop passer with a groove at one end designed to accommodate a thread loop, highlighting its use in the appendectomy procedure.
I have used this hand quilting needle that is having an eye at one end of the length of six point five centimeter and

shaved off the half length of this eye to make a groove and then joined these two needles with each other to make this full length low passer of thirteen centimeter that will be accommodated in twelve centimeter various needle
The surgeon removes the inner stylet from the varice needle, leaving only the outer needle part visible for use in the procedure.
The surgeon takes a full-length thread, creates a loop at its center, and feeds this loop into the groove of the loop passer instrument.

The surgeon uses the right hand to grab both ends of the thread and inserts the loop passer at the upper end of the needle. The loop passer emerges from the lower sharp end, and with the help of a grasper, the loop is grasped intraperitoneally before removing the loop passer.

and then you can feed the tip of the appendix through this loop and perform your maneuvers now i will demonstrate all these steps on a dummy to make your concepts clear but for live demonstration of this procedure there is a video link in the description kindly visit that link
A 10 mm optical trocar is inserted into the inferior umbilical crease, providing access for visualization during the appendectomy procedure.
A 5 mm trocar is inserted into the suprapubic region, providing access to the surgical field.
The surgeon inserts a varus needle into the right iliac fossa, targeting a point that allows optimal handling of the appendix during the procedure.
The surgeon removes the inner stylet from the varus needle after its insertion into the right iliac fossa.

The surgeon uses artery forceps to handle a loop, feeding it into the groove end of a loop passer. The loop passer is inserted into the varus needle until the loop reaches the peritoneal cavity, then the loop passer is removed.
The surgeon uses a grasper to manipulate the loop inside the peritoneal cavity, feeding extra length as needed. The grasper is then used to hold the tip of the appendix through the loop.

The surgeon slides a loop over the tip of the appendix using a varus needle and grasper, then tightens the loop by pulling it outward. The varus needle allows manipulation of the appendix tip in various directions, demonstrating its freedom of movement.

The surgeon works on the meso-appendix, performing dissection using either a ligature or an extracorporeal knot. If the appendix is long, the loop around the appendix is loosened to hold it further proximally for easier handling and manipulation. Once the dissection is complete, the loop is removed and extracted through the varus needle.
